title: The Drive to Establish Domestic HALEU Supply Chains is a Gambit - The National Interest
text:
The Drive to Establish Domestic HALEU Supply Chains is a Gambit
The United States is working to break Russia’s HALEU monopoly in order to ensure domestic energy security.
In a decisive move to revive America’s flagging nuclear sector, the US Department of Energy (DOE) has pledged to supply high-assay low-enriched uranium (HALEU) to five front-running nuclear developers.
This initiative marks a critical step in building a domestic fuel supply chain that is vital for the commercialization of advanced nuclear technologies, including small modular reactors (SMRs) and next-generation designs. The importance of HALEU, a nuclear fuel enriched between five percent and 20 percent uranium-235, lies in its superior performance characteristics, which enable smaller reactor sizes, longer operating cycles, and higher fuel efficiencies when compared to conventional low-enriched uranium (LEU) fuels.
In April 2025, the DOE issued its first conditional commitments to deliver HALEU to five developers, such as TRISO-X, Kairos Power, Radiant Industries, Westinghouse, and TerraPower. This dovetails with the United States’ broader national goals of securing energy independence, improving grid reliability, and reducing carbon emissions.
Energy Secretary Chris Wright underscored the significance, emphasizing that “allocating this HALEU material will help US nuclear developers deploy their advanced reactors with materials sourced from secure supply chains,” a vital countermeasure against reliance on foreign uranium sources, notably from Russia. Russia has been the world’s only commercial HALEU producer, a geopolitical vulnerability that Washington can no longer tolerate.
The HALEU Industry Must Overcome Production Bottlenecks
Although HALEU is indispensable for many emerging reactor designs, their domestic production capacity has historically lagged behind demand. The United States’ existing fuel enrichment infrastructure primarily supports LEU enriched to about three to five percent uranium-235, sufficient only for traditional reactors.
The demand for HALEU has rapidly increased in line with the resurgence of advanced nuclear reactors, exposing a significant shortfall in domestic production capacity. In 2024, only about 900 kilograms of HALEU were produced domestically, which is drastically lower than the projected annual demand expected to exceed 50 metric tons by 2035. The government manages HALEU stockpiles at Y-12, Idaho National Laboratory, and Savannah River, which are expected to reach about 21 metric tons by mid-2026, with around 14.9 metric tons potentially available as a temporary supply. This stockpile serves as an interim supply for early demonstrations but remains below DOE projections of approximately 40 metric tons needed by 2030 and over 50 metric tons annually by 2035, assuming planned SMR deployment.
The United States possesses an estimated 481 tons of unirradiated HEU, primarily retained for defense purposes, mostly accumulated from Cold War-era weapons programs. Civilian HEU production ceased in 1992, and no separated plutonium exists from non-military programs. With production reactors shuttered since 1987, repurposing defense stockpiles for HALEU via down-blending offers a pragmatic solution if policy allows.
US uranium production, once dominant, has plummeted. From its 1950s peak, supplies to commercial reactors dipped below 50 million pounds of triuranium octoxide by 2023, per the Energy Information Administration. Concentrate production hovered near zero in recent years, making the United States reliant on imports. The DOE’s HALEU Consortium seeks partnerships across the fuel cycle to sustain availability. The HALEU Availability Program, which was launched in 2020 under the Energy Act and funded with $700 million from the Inflation Reduction Act, represents the most significant federal intervention in civilian nuclear fuel since the 1960s. It mandates not only stockpile management but also the creation of the HALEU Consortium, a permanent public-private body that coordinates mining, conversion, enrichment, deconversion, and fabrication.
To close this gap, initiated in 2020, DOE’s HALEU Availability Program received approximately $700 million in funding from the Inflation Reduction Act to build a resilient domestic HALEU supply chain. Key milestones include partnerships with fuel fabrication entities like Standard Nuclear and joint ventures to establish scalable manufacturing facilities. These are expected to start operations by 2026 or 2027. These efforts are strategically aligned with DOE’s Advanced Reactor Demonstration Program and other risk reduction initiatives that are designed to fast-track reactor deployment with ready access to qualified fuel.
The 2024-25 DOE Fuel Line Pilot Program seeks to establish an intermediate-scale nuclear fuel manufacturing ecosystem in the United States to facilitate advanced reactor fuel supply. Five companies were selected: Standard Nuclear, Oklo, Terrestrial Energy, TRISO-X, and Valar Atomics. Each is authorized to source HALEU fuel from DOE stockpiles and develop the fuel manufacturing capacity to bridge research and commercial production.
HALEU’s Role in Advanced Reactors
Advanced reactor designs powered by HALEU offer multiple benefits over conventional nuclear technology. By increasing uranium-235 concentration to between five percent and 20 percent, HALEU fuels allow smaller reactor cores and extended fuel cycles, enhancing operational flexibility and economic competitiveness. Many SMR and microreactor designs leverage HALEU’s properties to deliver cleaner, more reliable power that is suited to diverse applications that range from grid resilience to remote and mobile power needs. Additionally, HALEU-enabled fuels improve the overall efficiency and safety of reactors while also reducing nuclear waste production. These attributes could expand nuclear energy’s role in a decarbonizing energy landscape, allowing it to support the United States’ commitments to reducing greenhouse gas emissions while maintaining energy security.
Geopolitical Imperatives Behind HALEU
Every pillar of the United States’ nuclear strategy is now shaped by one fact. Russia dominates global enrichment, controlling roughly forty percent of the world’s capacity and 100 percent of commercial HALEU production. Since 2022, Washington has banned the import of Russian uranium products, turning an already tight market into a strategic crisis.
The scarcity of HALEU production outside the United States, which is chiefly dominated by Russia, raises strategic and security concerns. Russia’s stronghold on the global enrichment market underscores the urgency behind the DOE’s drive to foster a wholly domestic nuclear fuel cycle.
This effort aims not only to fuel commercial reactors but also to mitigate vulnerabilities in national security, given uranium’s dual-use potential in civilian and military applications. For the United States’, the inability to fuel its own next-generation reactors is more than an industrial weakness. It is a national security liability.
The Dawn of a Resurgent US Nuclear Industry
The DOE’s commitment to increasing the HALEU supply embodies a broader strategy championed by the Trump administration and sustained by bipartisan political support regarding the revitalization of domestic nuclear fuel production and innovation. By expanding its enrichment and fabrication capacities, alongside technological investments in new reactor designs, the United States aims to cement its leadership in the next generation of nuclear energy.
The DOE’s strategy involves utilizing its stockpiles for near-term reactor needs, ramping up commercial capacity via pilot manufacturing lines, and encouraging private investment to scale production to tens of metric tons. The end goal is to ensure that, by the mid-2030s, domestic HALEU production is sufficient to support serial factory-built SMRs. That would mark a historic pivot from dependence on foreign enrichment to self-sufficiency in the world’s most strategically important clean-energy fuel.
The stakes are high. Without HALEU, the United States cannot lead the global SMR market.
About the Author: Stella Kim
Stella (SuHee) Kim is an investment and nuclear strategy expert with over a decade of experience bridging global finance and deep-tech, with a particular focus on small modular reactors (SMRs). She is the CEO of Pandia Bridge, a Singapore-based advisory firm that connects global investors and leading Korean conglomerates with top SMR developers, including NuScale Power, to facilitate cross-border investments and strategic partnerships. Her work centers on the intersection of energy security, technology innovation, and strategic finance on a global scale. She holds a BA from Ajou University in South Korea and participated in a study abroad program at the Luleå University of Technology in Sweden.
